title: Russia Unleashes Massive Ballistic Barrage on Ukraine, Burnham Becomes UK PM, Israeli Ministers March on Gaza
subhead: Russia fired Iskander and Zircon missiles across Ukraine killing at least eight, while Kyiv struck Russian oil infrastructure in response. Andy Burnham takes charge in London facing wars in Iran and Ukraine from day one. Israeli cabinet members joined a march demanding Gaza settlements be rebuilt.

sections.heading: Russia's Ballistic Missile Barrage Kills Eight Across Ukraine
sections.body:
Russia carried out one of the largest ballistic missile attacks of the war overnight, launching Iskander and hypersonic Zircon missiles along with 125 drones at Ukrainian cities. At least eight people were killed: four in Kharkiv, one in Kyiv, two in Zaporizhzhia, and one in Sumy. Kyiv's air defences shot down 18 of 41 missiles and intercepted 108 drones, but impacts damaged residential buildings, a supermarket, a dormitory, and a logistics hub in the Bucha district, according to the BBC.

President Volodymyr Zelensky called it 'one of the most massive ballistic attacks' since the full-scale invasion began. He said Russia had used approximately 1,450 strike drones, more than 1,640 guided bombs, and 99 missiles of various types against Ukraine in the past week alone. He appealed for increased supplies of ballistic missile interceptors from European allies.

Ukraine struck back hard. Its SBU security service hit three oil depots in Russia's Stavropol region simultaneously, while Ukrainian forces struck three Russian 'shadow fleet' oil tankers in the Black Sea. The Caspian Pipeline Consortium confirmed its Novorossiysk terminal was hit, damaging two tankers and suspending oil loading operations. Ukrainian attacks also killed seven at a Wildberries warehouse in Tambov and one at another facility near Moscow, according to the BBC.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale of Russia's missile barrage and Ukraine's deep strikes on Russian oil infrastructure show both sides escalating the air war. Ukraine's targeting of Russia's fuel supply chain — now disabling an estimated 43% of refining capacity — is systematically eroding Moscow's ability to fund the war.

sections.heading: US-Iran War Enters Ninth Night as Third Soldier Killed
sections.body:
The United States struck Iran for a ninth consecutive night, with explosions reported in the port cities of Mahshahr and Imam Khomeini in Khuzestan province, near Sirik and Jask in Hormozgan province, and for the first time in the northwestern city of Tabriz, according to Iran's IRIB state broadcaster. The US military said the strikes targeted Iranian military coastal surveillance, air defence facilities, maritime capabilities, and missile and drone storage sites.

A third US service member was killed in northern Iraq during a controlled detonation of ordnance from a downed Iranian drone, CENTCOM announced. Two soldiers had been killed in Friday's Iranian ballistic missile attack on a US base in Jordan; CENTCOM also said 'unidentified remains' had been found at that site. The total US death toll since the war began has reached 17, with more than 420 wounded.

Iran struck back at US allies in the region. Kuwait reported a desalination and power plant hit for the second day in a row, causing a fire. Bahrain said it thwarted Iranian missile-and-drone attacks. Jordan shot down three of four Iranian missiles, summoning Iran's ambassador. Iran's IRGC said two vessels that ignored warnings in the Strait of Hormuz were involved in 'accidents,' while the US said it had redirected six commercial vessels and disabled another to enforce its naval blockade on Iranian ports.

Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, who has not been seen publicly since assuming the role in March, issued a written statement calling for 'sacred unity' and warning that the US president's signature was 'utterly worthless and devoid of credibility.' The statement appeared aimed as much at domestic hardliners who have accused officials of treason over the collapsed MoU as at Washington.
sections.whyItMatters: The conflict is expanding geographically — US strikes reached Tabriz in Iran's northwest for the first time, while Iran's retaliation now routinely hits civilian infrastructure in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan. Neither side shows interest in returning to negotiations.

sections.heading: Andy Burnham Becomes UK Prime Minister Monday
sections.body:
Andy Burnham will be sworn in as the United Kingdom's seventh prime minister in a decade on Monday, taking over from Keir Starmer after winning the Labour leadership in a rapid contest following Starmer's resignation last month. Al Jazeera reports that Burnham's in-tray is formidable: an economy that has barely grown in two decades, a trailing position behind Reform UK in the polls, and wars in both Ukraine and Iran demanding immediate attention.

The British Steel nationalisation completed last week adds a diplomatic dimension — China's Foreign Ministry warned that how London handles the matter will 'directly affect Chinese investors' confidence' and 'shape public perceptions in China of the British government's credibility.' Jingye Group, British Steel's former Chinese owner, is seeking 'full compensation through legal means' for the nationalisation of the Scunthorpe works, Britain's only primary steelmaking facility. Anand Menon of UK in a Changing Europe noted Burnham will have 'relatively little fiscal room for manoeuvre,' with government borrowing higher than planned and taxes forecast to reach their highest share of the economy since World War II.

On foreign policy, Burnham is expected to prioritise defence spending over welfare and may allow new North Sea oil and gas drilling, according to analysts cited by Al Jazeera. He has apologised for the previous government's stance on Gaza, suggesting a possible shift, but Menon cautioned that turning words into policy 'depends heavily on events.' Burnham is unlikely to risk a rupture with Washington over US bases in Britain used in the Iran war.
sections.whyItMatters: Burnham inherits a country squeezed between fiscal constraints, a China row over steel nationalisation, and two active wars requiring immediate decisions. His choices on defence spending, energy policy, and Gaza will set the tone for a premiership that starts under immense pressure.

sections.heading: Israeli Cabinet Ministers Join March Demanding Gaza Settlements
sections.body:
Israeli cabinet ministers Bezalel Smotrich and Itamar Ben-Gvir joined hundreds of marchers heading toward Gaza's border on Sunday to demand the rebuilding of Jewish settlements dismantled in 2005. The march, organised by the settler group Nachala under the banner 'returning home after 21 years,' called for reconstructing the settlements of Elei Sinai, Dugit, and Nisanit in northern Gaza. Netanyahu's Likud party was listed among the rally's sponsors, despite the prime minister's insistence that Gaza will not be resettled.

Al Jazeera reports that Defence Minister Israel Katz recently told Channel 14 he intends to establish three 'Nahal outposts' — military entities combining farming with armed presence — in northern Gaza. Smotrich, who also holds the settlement administration portfolio, said the groundwork for three settlements had been completed. Palestinian human rights defender Issam Younis of the Al Mezan Center described the push as part of a broader strategy: 'take over land, starve Gaza and deny it any chance at life, then expel the population and build settlements.'

Nine months into a US-brokered ceasefire, Israeli attacks have killed at least 1,127 Palestinians in Gaza, including 265 children, according to the UN. Israel has expanded the 'Yellow Line' demarcating its controlled areas to encompass 70 percent of the Strip, with Katz declaring 'we are not retreating.' Meanwhile, Lebanon's President Joseph Aoun met Secretary of State Marco Rubio ahead of expected talks with Trump on Tuesday to discuss ending Israel-Hezbollah fighting.
sections.whyItMatters: Sitting cabinet ministers openly advocating for Gaza settlements — with Likud as a listed sponsor — signals that resettlement has moved from the fringe into mainstream Israeli policy discourse. With Israel heading to elections on October 27, the settlement push is likely to intensify.

sections.heading: Hungary's Orbán-Era President Resigns Under Constitutional Pressure
sections.body:
President Tamás Sulyok signed a constitutional amendment ending his presidency at midnight Saturday, stepping down under pressure from Prime Minister Péter Magyar's Tisza party, which pushed the law through parliament after its landslide victory in April. Sulyok, widely seen as a loyalist of former prime minister Viktor Orbán, accused the new government of reaching 'a breaking point in Hungarian constitutional democracy' and trampling 'the core values of a free society... for the sake of political power.'

The amendment also removes the head of the Constitutional Court and bars MPs who have served three terms from standing again — which applies to more than half of Orbán's Fidesz party deputies. Orbán, who has rarely been seen in public since his shock defeat, described the amendment as tyranny and called for protests. Former Supreme Court head András Baka told the BBC it was 'very difficult to break up a sophisticated authoritarian regime... which was designed to survive even after electoral defeat,' arguing Sulyok's removal was justified.
sections.whyItMatters: Magyar's government is rapidly dismantling Orbán's institutional framework — the presidency, constitutional court, and parliamentary immunity for Fidesz MPs all in one amendment. The question is whether democratic restoration or constitutional overreach, and whether Fidesz can rebuild as opposition.

sections.heading: Peru Hit by Twin Earthquakes, Guyana Ferry Capsizes, Norway Wildfire Rages
sections.body:
Two earthquakes measuring 5.1 and 3.7 magnitude struck Peru's mountainous Junin region east of Lima on Saturday, killing at least five people and injuring 21. At least 48 homes were destroyed and 18 damaged, displacing about 300 people. President-elect Keiko Fujimori expressed condolences. The adobe construction common in the area amplified the damage.

In Guyana, the MV Barima ferry carrying 116 passengers and 17 crew capsized near Iron Punt after departing Georgetown. Sixty-seven people have been rescued, including 15 children, but dozens remain missing. The vessel, built in 1939 and within its weight capacity, was equipped with 250 life jackets. Prime Minister Mark Phillips said search areas had been 'significantly increased.'

In Norway, a wildfire in Drammen has destroyed more than 100 homes and spread to a nearby forest. Helicopters have been dumping water on the blaze as authorities struggle to contain it. The fire comes amid extreme wildfire conditions across southern Europe and North America, with Canada's ongoing wildfires also sending smoke haze over the US east coast — the MetLife World Cup final stadium in New Jersey was shrouded in smoky haze during Sunday's match.
sections.whyItMatters: From Peru's adobe villages to Norway's suburbs to the World Cup final shrouded in Canadian wildfire smoke, the cascading impacts of natural disasters and environmental crises are compounding the geopolitical stress of 2026.

bottomLine: Russia escalated its air war on Ukraine with one of the largest ballistic missile barrages of the conflict, while Ukraine struck back at Russian oil infrastructure and shadow fleet tankers in the Black Sea. The US-Iran war ground into its ninth night with strikes reaching Tabriz for the first time and a third American soldier killed, as Iran hit Kuwaiti desalination plants and Gulf state allies for a second day. Andy Burnham takes over as UK prime minister on Monday facing an immediate China dispute over British Steel and two wars demanding attention. Israeli cabinet ministers openly marched on Gaza's border demanding settlements, and Hungary's post-Orbán government forced out the last president — another institution reshaped in the new order's image.
